title: Portland City Council Homeless & Housing Committee 03/10/26

chapters.summary: Good afternoon. I call the meeting of Home Assistance and Housing Committee to order on March 10th at 12:03 PM. Claire, uh, Claire, please read the statement of conduct. Councilor Ryan, if you can hear us online. Absent.
chapters.headline: Home Assistance and Housing Committee to meet March 10 at 12:03 PM
chapters.gist: Meeting of Home Assistance and Housing Committee

chapters.summary: To testify before this committee in person or virtually, you must sign up in advance on the committee agenda at portland. gov/council/agenda/homelessnessandhousingcommittee or by calling 311. Further disruption will result in ejection from the meeting.
chapters.headline: To testify before this committee in person or virtually, you must sign up
chapters.gist: Committee on Homelessness and Housing Meeting

chapters.summary: Today we have two items. Chair Avalos, I'm here. Councilor Ryan. Uh-huh. Got it. Thank you.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan Avalos is presenting today's agenda

chapters.summary: First, we're going to hear from members of the public on their feedback on homelessness and housing issues. 8 people signed up to testify today. Then we will hear an update from Portland Permitting and Development on how the temporary SDC exemption is working so far.
chapters.headline: We will hear from community members on homelessness and housing issues today
chapters.gist: Portland City Council: Homeless and Housing Community Feedback

chapters.summary: Robert Pyle: I'm going to urge you to put at least some of the newfound money that you guys have might be put towards housing assistance. He says we need to focus on behavioral wellness at the Home Forward facilities. Pyle asks that Home Forward and the other affordable housing agencies require behavioral wellness education.
chapters.headline: Robert Pyle: Home Forward and other affordable housing agencies should focus on behavioral wellness

chapters.summary: Only 265 units of new housing have complied with the requirements to receive SDC exemptions. Housing production is economic and workforce development. Every dollar spent on construction returns 3 times to our local economy. Please request PHP to enroll Portland as a sponsoring jurisdiction in this program as soon as possible.
chapters.headline: Only 265 units of new housing have complied with SDC exemptions in last 6 months
chapters.gist: Building more affordable housing in Portland

chapters.summary: Next is Bob Law. Bob, if you can hear us, feel free to unmute. Next is Dr. Ernesto Fonseca. Sahan McKelvey, Stephanie Condor. I think they might be experiencing technical issues. We'll circle back.
chapters.headline: Next is Bob Law. Bob, if you can hear us, feel free to unmute and begin your testimony

chapters.summary: Chair of the committee: I want to talk to you about a project that is in North Portland that was abandoned by the original developer. Chair: Those of us online cannot hear the current person speaking. All right, why don't we restart the clock for Ernesto?
chapters.headline: Chair, those of us online cannot hear the current person speaking
chapters.gist: Chair of the Committee Discusses North Portland Project

chapters.summary: A developer defaulted on a 72-unit affordable housing project in mid-2024. Hacienda has redesigned the project into 57 larger family units. The state has allocated another $13 million for us to finish the project. But we need a final investment support of $3.5 million.
chapters.headline: Hacienda is working to renovate an affordable housing project in Portland
chapters.gist: Please help us finish this affordable housing project!

chapters.summary: Saham McKelvey is the Director of Advocacy and Engagement at Self Enhancement, Inc. He spoke to the committee regarding the THB funds that are currently being discussed in the full council. He urged the committee to determine the most viable path forward that can maximize the impact of these dollars in our community.
chapters.headline: Saham McKelvey speaks to Housing Committee regarding THB funds
chapters.gist: Saham McKelvey on the THB Funds

chapters.summary: I would also urge you to remember that for the thousands of people who are living in homelessness or dealing with the day-to-day trauma of pending eviction. You have the power to make some people's dreams come true. Please do not delay in doing so.
chapters.headline: I would also urge you to remember that for the thousands of people living in homelessness

chapters.summary: Broadway Corridor Parcel 6 will deliver more than 200 homes and will be 100% income restricted. Addressing our housing and affordability challenges requires a balanced approach. We must pair assistance with the creation of new housing opportunities.
chapters.headline: Steph Condor is the lead developer behind Broadway Corridor Parcel 6

chapters.summary: The Burnside project is an affordable housing SDC-waivered project. The difficulty in getting people to qualify is substantial, especially for the 3-bedroom bonus program. The typical person is going to need pretty much family help in order to qualify.
chapters.headline: The difficulty in getting people to qualify is substantial for the 3-bedroom bonus program
chapters.gist: Affordable Housing and the SDC Waivered Program

chapters.summary: Dakota, Lisa Jackson Spotted Elk, Minthorn Norma and Frank Kadamiya are on the list. They want to address housing in their communities. Who's holding nonprofits accountable? This is going to take generations to change, they say.
chapters.headline: Dakota wants to address housing issues for Native Americans
chapters.gist: Native Americans on the Homelessness Council

chapters.summary: John Collins: I'm concerned about the way I've been treated as a veteran in these homeless centers. Collins: A lot of veterans that I meet are very annoyed at how they're being treated. Is there anything that you can do to help?
chapters.headline: John Collins is concerned about the way homeless centers are treating veterans
chapters.gist: Testifier 1: Concerns about homeless and veterans

chapters.summary: John: We just found out we had $107 million in unspent funding. Also heard the announcement from the county that they're looking to cut about 700 beds and life-saving resources. Would highly recommend that we look at using some of this money with the city to rescue one or two shelters.
chapters.headline: John: County is looking to cut 700 shelter beds and life-saving resources
chapters.gist: John on the Need for Housing

chapters.summary: Staff from Portland Permitting and Development will provide an update on the temporary SDC exemption program for new housing. Program was adopted last year with the goal of reducing upfront development costs. Presentation will walk us through the early implementation and the initial data where beginning to see.
chapters.headline: Portland staff will provide an update on temporary SDC exemption program for new housing
chapters.gist: Portland Permitting and Development Update on the Temporary SDC Exemption

chapters.summary: System development charges, or SDCs, are a type of fee the city charges to development projects. The ordinance created a new category of SDC exemption, essentially waiving those charges for development projects that build new housing. PP&D is working through the process to convert ENB 13.29 into a permanent rule.
chapters.headline: System development charges are a fee the city charges to development projects

chapters.summary: Every aspect of this program's implementation has benefited from significant partnership between city bureaus. No one bureau or office in the city centrally manages all aspects of SDCs. It's taken a village and hundreds of hours of staff time to get here.
chapters.headline: Every aspect of this program has benefited from significant partnership between city bureaus

chapters.summary: Council requested written implementation reports every 6 months. Bullet 4 asks for identification of material changes in the rate or timing of permit issuance and construction starts. For this first report, we opted to share anecdotal feedback from housing developers. Alice will share that feedback later on in the presentation.
chapters.headline: Council requested written implementation reports every 6 months on specific topics

chapters.summary: Alice Nielsen: Up next, we have the first analytic snapshot of this program. We will go over the current milestone, which includes the initial 5-month implementation period. The next written report will be in the fall of 2026 and will include a full year of data.
chapters.headline: Alice Nielsen: We have the first analytic snapshot of this program

chapters.summary: Qualifying SDC exemptions are tracked through the following statuses: enrolled, issued, and complied. Complied status refers to permitted units that have met the required construction milestones, resulting in the permanent exemption of SDC fees.
chapters.headline: Qualifying SDC exemptions are tracked through the following statuses: enrolled, issued
chapters.gist: Building Permits Under the SDC Program

chapters.summary: 34.4% of our goal is active within the pipeline. 265 new housing units have complied with the program for an approximate total of $6.6 million in SDC exemptions. There are currently 3,280 units, or 65.6%, remaining to reach the 5,000-unit goal.
chapters.headline: This slide shows the current progress towards our 5,000-unit goal

chapters.summary: The primary driver of units is apartments, with 878 units enrolled, 159 units issued, and 41 units in compliance. Next up, we have units broken down by housing type and how each type is contributing to the 5,000-unit goal.
chapters.headline: Next slide shows units broken down by housing type and how each type contributes

chapters.summary: The number of new units saw an increase in October of 2025, reaching a total of 153 units. Spikes in the unit count are attributable for the most part due, due to apartment developments. Some permits that have been ready to approve for several months or years but waiting to pay their fees have since indicated movement forward.
chapters.headline: Number of new housing permits created through issued building permits increased in 2025

chapters.summary: There are a few economic factors that continue to present difficulties for the construction industry. Development costs have surged by approximately 50% since the onset of the pandemic. The next factor is marginal interest rate fluctuations. It is still early to tell how successful this program will be.
chapters.headline: There are several economic factors that continue to present difficulties for the construction industry

chapters.summary: We are working towards a permanent administrative rule which will be effective in May of 2026. The next written report will be delivered in the fall of 20 26 and should give us much clearer analytics as to how this program is helping or not helping build new housing units.
chapters.headline: I reached out to several developers to see if SDC exemption is helping projects

chapters.summary: The report was very helpful overall and I think can give us some context as far as decision-making around this moving forward. We've been kicking around the idea of a survey for our customers. It should be pretty easy for us to put together a survey and send it out.
chapters.headline: I thought the report was very helpful overall and I think can give us some context

chapters.summary: I was also wondering, do we have any examples of specific infrastructure projects that had to be moved up or expanded due to new developments that were exempted from SDCs? And also, I can send these in an email later if that's helpful.
chapters.headline: I was also wondering, do we have any examples of specific infrastructure projects that have
chapters.gist: Specific Infrastructure Projects Due to SEC-Exempted Development

chapters.summary: Councilor: Is it possible to look at cottage cluster type housing or middle housing and have it broken out from the general family or single-family home category? Councilor: Most of that money was allocated for long-term use. About $21 million million for council to address that is unallocated right now.
chapters.headline: Councilor Dempsey asks if it's possible to disaggregate housing data
chapters.gist: Cottage Cluster Housing Reporting in the Next Report

chapters.summary: The number of issued and complied permits is dramatically smaller than the number enrolled. Is that to be expected? The typical development cycle, regardless of SDCs, slows down in the wintertime. The larger projects are just taking longer to figure out right now.
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo asked about backlog created by SDC waiver

chapters.summary: Council President: There is some conflict between this SDC waiver program and the affordable housing SDC waivers program. Housing Bureau is working on a policy policy to address that. If council needs to take more action to make this work better, I am eager to see what that looks like.
chapters.headline: Council President: There is conflict between SDC waiver and affordable housing exemption
chapters.gist: Council President Ana Leyva on the SDC Waiver Program

chapters.summary: The administrative rule is moving from temporary to permanent. What specifically would change by making the rule permanent? And what would be the downside of keeping the rule temporary until we have more data?
chapters.headline: The administrative rule is moving from temporary to permanent status

chapters.summary: How are we balancing housing incentives with maintaining this infrastructure that residents rely on? That's a great question. I think it's a policy decision. I stand by the decision I made to support the SEC waiver, but I have to continue to raise that.
chapters.headline: How are we balancing housing incentives with maintaining infrastructure that residents rely on
chapters.gist: Portland City Council: SDCs and Fiscal Impact

chapters.summary: There's a lot of interest in the non-District 4 districts. In October, we saw a 38% increase over October '24. Bucking trends is important. Those of you that are dancing in the periphery, it's time to get in the ring.
chapters.headline: Councilor Zimmerman: We saw 38% increase in permits in October

chapters.summary: Construction is up and down all the time. It goes on cycles. It definitely does show how very busy we were at certain points. So we're hoping that it'll start trending upward again.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan asks question on issued permit unit count on slide 13

chapters.summary: Mayor: What we're hearing is that this is working for smaller and mid-sized projects. But it's a lot harder for the larger projects. So larger projects obviously take longer to build. And we may need to bump those timelines out.
chapters.headline: Stephen and Alice, what have you learned about this program so far
chapters.gist: Council Member Murillo on the City's Infrastructure Program

chapters.summary: Would you say most of the pull permits are for under-19 units? There, I would say yes. A lot of them are like cottage clusters, middle housing, things like that. I think it's showing promise. Let's keep figuring out how we can keep improving upon this.
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo says most pull permits are for under 19 units

chapters.summary: This is the last meeting of this iteration of the Housing Committee. Meeting is adjourned at 1:32 PM. Thank you all for your service. See you next time.
chapters.headline: Last call for any more questions or comments is adjourned at 1:32 PM
